<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt"><B>BancWest Corporation and Subsidiaries<BR>
MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL<BR>
CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B>

</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The increase in advertising and promotion expenses was due to higher advertising activities in
2004 related to deposit campaigns and campaigns to promote brand recognition.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The $16.1&nbsp;million in restructuring and integration costs for 2004 were related to the
acquisitions of Community First and USDB and were comprised of $10.9&nbsp;million in contracted
services, $1.7&nbsp;million in travel related expenses, $1.6&nbsp;million in stationery and supplies, $0.6
million in salaries and benefits, $0.6&nbsp;million in advertising and promotions and $0.7&nbsp;million in
other miscellaneous expenses.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The increase in other noninterest expense was partially due to higher fees resulting from
increased transaction volume related to airline branded credit cards, significantly offset by a
decrease in the residual value of certain leveraged leases in 2003 and lower split dollar life
insurance expenses as a result of marking policies to cash surrender value.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left">
<A name="129"></A>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>OPERATING SEGMENTS</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Our reportable segments are the operating segments that we use in our internal reporting at
Bank of the West and First Hawaiian Bank. Bank of the West&#146;s segments operate primarily in
Arizona, California, Colorado, Idaho, Iowa, Kansas, Minnesota, Missouri, Nebraska, Nevada, New
Mexico, North Dakota, Oklahoma, Oregon, South Dakota, Utah, Washington, Wisconsin and Wyoming.
Certain Bank of the West segments conduct business nationwide. Although First Hawaiian Bank&#146;s
segments operate primarily in Hawaii, it also has significant operations outside the state, such as
leveraged leasing and branches in Guam and Saipan. It also has significant operations extending to
California through its automobile dealer flooring and financing activities.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>Bank of the West</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2005 compared with 2004</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Bank of the West&#146;s net income increased to $525.6&nbsp;million, up $106.5&nbsp;million, or 25.4%. Net
interest income increased $256.4&nbsp;million or 22.2%, primarily due to higher balances in earning
assets from the acquisitions of Community First, USDB and Commercial Federal. Noninterest income
increased $107.9&nbsp;million, or 37.5%. The increase was due to increases in service charges on
deposit accounts, debit card interchange revenue, brokerage service fees, insurance commissions and
trust and syndication fees. These increases were primarily the result of the acquisitions in 2004
of Community First and USDB and to some extent the 2005 Commercial Federal acquisition. Noninterest
expense increased $219.6&nbsp;million, or 30.4%. The increase was primarily due to increases in
salaries and benefits and direct occupancy costs related to the 2004 and 2005 acquisitions along
with additional expenses related to two de nova branches. The provision for credit losses decreased
by $12.3&nbsp;million.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Average
assets increased $11.0&nbsp;billion to $42.9&nbsp;billion. Average loans increased by $6.5
billion, or 28.8%, predominately due to the acquisitions and purchases of residential loans.
Average deposits increased $6.4&nbsp;billion, or 30.1%, predominately due to the acquisitions and an
increase in short-term certificates of deposits.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2004 compared with 2003</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Bank
of the West&#146;s net income increased to $419.1&nbsp;million, up $28.3&nbsp;million, or 7.2%. Net
interest income increased $62.7&nbsp;million, or 5.7%, primarily due to higher balances in earning
assets resulting from the acquisitions of Community First and USDB. Noninterest income increased
$41.8&nbsp;million, or 17.0%. The increase is mostly due to an increase in commission fees, syndication
fees, gains on the sale of Small Business Administration (&#147;SBA&#148;) loans and recording lease payments
under the operating lease method of accounting from February to July&nbsp;2004, offset by a decrease in
service charges and SBA servicing income. Noninterest expense increased $89.7&nbsp;million, or 14.2%.
The provision for credit losses decreased by $31.1&nbsp;million.
</DIV>


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">28
</DIV>


<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>
<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">








<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt"><B>BancWest Corporation and Subsidiaries<BR>
MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL<BR>
CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B>

</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<B>Regional Banking</B>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2005 compared with 2004</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Regional Banking segment&#146;s net income increased $75.4&nbsp;million, or 54.8% from $137.5
million to $212.9&nbsp;million. Net interest income increased $252.2&nbsp;million or 49.4% from last year.
The increase is primarily due to the growth in average loans outstanding due to the full year
effect of the Community First and USDB acquisitions in November&nbsp;2004. Additionally the overall
interest margin on liabilities increased 41 basis points from 2004 while the interest margin on
loans decreased 7 basis points. Noninterest income increased $90.6&nbsp;million or 51.3%. The increase
is predominately due to increased service charges on deposit accounts, an increase in debit card
interchange revenue and increases in brokerage service and insurance commission fees. Noninterest
expense increased $215.7&nbsp;million or 47.3%. The increase is primarily due to an increase in
compensation expenses and direct occupancy costs related to branches added with the acquisitions of
Community First, USDB and Commercial Federal.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Average loans and leases increased $4.5&nbsp;billion or 71.3%. The increase is primarily due to
real estate residential loan purchases throughout the year and from the Community First acquisition
in the fourth quarter of 2004.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Average deposits increased $5.4&nbsp;billion or 36.0%. The increase is primarily due to growth in
core deposits and the Community First acquisition in 2004.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2004 compared with 2003</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Regional Banking segment&#146;s net income increased $1.8&nbsp;million, or 1.3%, from $135.7&nbsp;million
to $137.5&nbsp;million in 2004 as compared with 2003. Net interest income increased $15.1&nbsp;million or
3.1% from the prior year. The increase is primarily related to a larger transfer pricing
adjustment in 2004, offset by a 68 basis point decrease in the margin on demand deposits.
Noninterest income increased $13.4&nbsp;million or 8.2%. The increase is primarily due to increased
service charges on deposit accounts, an increase in debit card interchange revenue and investment
sales fees. Noninterest expense increased $32.6&nbsp;million or 7.7%. The increase is primarily due to
an increase in compensation expenses, direct occupancy costs related to two de novo branches and
increased third party vendor contracts.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<B>Commercial Banking</B>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2005 compared with 2004</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Commercial Banking segment&#146;s net income increased to $186.7&nbsp;million, up $33.9&nbsp;million, or
22.2%, from $152.8&nbsp;million. Net interest income increased $37.1&nbsp;million, or 11.6% due to increases
in loans and leases, partially offset by increases in deposits, rates paid on deposits and lower
margins on loans and leases. Noninterest income increased $12.5&nbsp;million, or 18.4%. The increase is
primarily related to increased trust and syndication fees, partially offset by a decrease in asset
management fees due to the closure of Eureka Investment Advisors in the fourth quarter of 2004.
Noninterest expense increased $9.9&nbsp;million, or 7.3%. The increase is partly due to higher
compensation and employee healthcare benefits resulting from the acquisitions and internal growth.
The provision for credit losses decreased by $13.5&nbsp;million in 2005, primarily related to an
improvement in credit quality and an increase in net recoveries.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Average loans and leases in 2005 increased 20.5% from 2004 to $9.3&nbsp;billion. The increase was
partly due to new equipment leases and SBA lending. The interest margin on loans and leases
decreased 20 basis points to 2.54% during 2005 due to declining margins in all product categories.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Average deposits increased 24.6% to $4.5&nbsp;billion in 2005. The increase was partly due to
higher time deposits and short-term negotiable CD&#146;s. The deposit margin increased 9 basis points
to 1.75% in 2005. The increase in the deposit margin from the prior year is due to an increase in
transfer pricing on demand deposit accounts, which increased by $312&nbsp;million, or 20.0%, with much
of the increase related to title company activity.
</DIV>

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">29
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt"><B>BancWest Corporation and Subsidiaries<BR>
MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL<BR>
CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B>

</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2004 compared with 2003</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Commercial Banking segment&#146;s net income decreased to $152.8&nbsp;million in 2004, down $1.1
million, or 0.7%, from $153.9&nbsp;million in 2003. Net interest income increased $2.9&nbsp;million, or
0.9%. Noninterest income increased $19.7&nbsp;million, or 40.8%. The increase is partially related to
increased commission fees, syndication fees and gains on the sale of SBA loans from the growth in
the SBA portfolio, partially offset by decreased service charges and Small Business Administration
(SBA)&nbsp;servicing income. Noninterest expense increased $17.7&nbsp;million, or 15.1%. The increase is
partly due to higher compensation and employee healthcare benefits. Provision for credit losses
increased by $2.0&nbsp;million in 2004 to $1.4&nbsp;million primarily related to a large recovery in 2003.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<B>Consumer Finance</B>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2005 compared with 2004</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Consumer Finance segment&#146;s net income increased $16.6&nbsp;million, or 22.0% to $92.2&nbsp;million
compared to $75.6&nbsp;million in 2004. Net interest income was $233.6&nbsp;million, compared with $212.2
million in 2004, an increase of 10.1%, primarily due to the increase in earning assets that
resulted from the 2004 acquisitions. Noninterest income increased $5.6&nbsp;million, or 23.0% to $30.0
million. The increase is partially due to recording lease payments as noninterest income for all
auto leases recorded under the operating method of accounting from February through July&nbsp;2004.
Noninterest expense increased $4.7&nbsp;million to $86.2&nbsp;million in 2005. The increase is due primarily
to higher employee salaries and healthcare benefits due to increased staffing from the acquisitions
and internal growth, as well as an increase in operating lease expense due to a full year of
depreciation under the operating method of accounting for auto leases. These increases were offset
by a reduction in certain allocated costs. The provision for credit losses decreased $4.5&nbsp;million
from $29.5&nbsp;million in 2004 to $25.0&nbsp;million in 2005, due to an improvement in credit quality.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Average assets in 2005 were $9.4&nbsp;billion compared to $8.5&nbsp;billion in 2004, an increase of
10.4%. This increase is due to increased indirect loan production and the addition of assets from
the acquisition of Community First in the fourth quarter of 2004.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2004 compared with 2003</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Consumer Finance segment&#146;s net income increased $12.6&nbsp;million in 2004, or 20.0% to $75.6
million compared to $63.0&nbsp;million in 2003. Net interest income was $212.2&nbsp;million, compared to
$207.1&nbsp;million in 2003, an increase of 2.5%. Noninterest income increased $12.6&nbsp;million, or 106.8%
to $24.4&nbsp;million. The increase is partially due to recording lease payments as noninterest income
for all auto leases recorded under the operating method of accounting from February through July
2004. This increase was partially offset by lower gains on sales of loans through our Essex
subsidiary, which were down $5.0&nbsp;million in 2004. In February&nbsp;2004, Essex began retaining a
percentage of new loan originations in its own portfolio. In previous years, Essex sold 100% of
its loan originations. Noninterest expense increased $21.2&nbsp;million to $81.5&nbsp;million in 2004 as
compared with 2003. The increase is due primarily to higher employee salaries and healthcare
benefits, a decrease in deferred loan origination costs and higher depreciation expense as a result
of the accounting for certain vehicle leases as operating leases. The provision for credit losses
decreased $25.1&nbsp;million from $54.6&nbsp;million in 2003 to $29.5&nbsp;million in 2004, due to an improvement
in credit quality.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>First Hawaiian Bank</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2005 compared with 2004</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;First Hawaiian Bank&#146;s net income increased to $171.9&nbsp;million, up $28.3&nbsp;million, or 19.7%. Net
interest income increased $53.9&nbsp;million, or 16.3%, primarily due to higher balances of earning
assets and an increase in the net interest margin. The increase in earning assets was primarily in
loans and leases and investment securities and was funded by growth in deposits, primarily time and
demand deposits. The increase in the net interest margin was primarily due to the Bank&#146;s ability
to control rates paid on deposit accounts in the rising interest rate environment and high growth
in noninterest bearing demand deposits.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Noninterest income decreased $4.1&nbsp;million, or 2.8%, primarily due to a gain on the sale of a
lease in 2004 and a decrease in account analysis fees resulting from higher earnings credit rates
in 2005. The decreases were partially offset by an increase in income earned on bank owned life
insurance and debit card interchange fees due to an increase in volume in 2005.
</DIV>

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">30
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt"><B>BancWest Corporation and Subsidiaries<BR>
MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL<BR>
CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B>

</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Noninterest expense increased $9.2&nbsp;million, or 4.1%, primarily due to increases in occupancy
and furniture and equipment expense resulting from higher levels of building maintenance and
depreciation on the branch platform automation system.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Average assets increased 10.5% to $11.0&nbsp;billion, largely a result of increased loans and
investment securities. Average loans increased by $0.5&nbsp;billion, or 9.4%, resulting from growth in
commercial and residential real estate and consumer loans. Average deposits increased $0.8
billion, or 11.2%, primarily due to an increase in demand and time deposits.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2004 compared with 2003</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;First
Hawaiian Bank&#146;s net income increased to $143.6&nbsp;million, up
$7.6&nbsp;million, or 5.6%. Net
interest income decreased $1.9&nbsp;million to $329.8&nbsp;million. The decrease in net interest income was
a result of declining yields earned on loans and leases and other earning assets which outpaced the
decrease in deposit costs. This decrease was partially offset by an increase in earning assets,
primarily loans and leases and investment securities, which was funded by increased core deposits.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Noninterest income remained relatively constant, decreasing by $0.7&nbsp;million. The decrease in
gains on sale of mortgage loans and certain other assets in 2003 were offset by a gain in sale of a
certain leveraged lease in 2004.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Noninterest expense decreased $15.0&nbsp;million, or 6.2%, primarily due to lower salaries and
benefits and occupancy costs in 2004. These decreases were partially offset by an accrual for
estimated losses related to credit card transactions processed on behalf of an airline which had
filed a voluntary petition to reorganize under Chapter&nbsp;11 of the Bankruptcy Code in December&nbsp;2004.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Average assets increased 6.0% to $9.9&nbsp;billion, primarily as a result of increased loans and
investment securities. Average loans increased by $226&nbsp;million, or 4.5%. Average deposits
increased $573&nbsp;million, or 8.4%, primarily due to an increase in demand deposits and savings and
time deposits.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<B>Retail Banking</B>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2005 compared with 2004</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Retail Banking segment&#146;s net income increased to $110.7&nbsp;million, up $34.1&nbsp;million, or
44.5%. Net interest income increased $51.3&nbsp;million, or 21.0%, primarily due to higher balances in
earning assets. Noninterest income decreased $1.5&nbsp;million, or 2.5%. Noninterest expense increased
$1.4&nbsp;million compared to the prior year. The provision for credit losses decreased $2.7&nbsp;million,
or 55.1%. The decrease in the provision for credit losses was a result of recoveries in 2005.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Average assets increased 11.3% to $4.2&nbsp;billion, primarily due to increases in loans of $0.4
billion. The increase in loans was primarily in residential and commercial real estate. Average
deposits increased 10.9% to $7.9&nbsp;billion, primarily due to an increase in demand, savings and time
deposits.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2004 compared with 2003</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Retail Banking segment&#146;s net income increased to $76.6&nbsp;million, up $8.1&nbsp;million, or 11.8%.
Net interest income increased $14.7&nbsp;million, or 6.4%, primarily due to higher earning asset
balances. Noninterest income remained the same at $59.6&nbsp;million. Noninterest expense increased
due to higher allocated expenses, partially offset by a decrease in occupancy expense corresponding
to the purchase of the First Hawaiian Center in December&nbsp;2003. The provision for credit losses
decreased $1.4&nbsp;million, or 22.2%.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<B>Consumer Finance</B>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2005 compared with 2004</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Average assets increased 4.6% to $1.6&nbsp;billion, partly due to increases in consumer and dealer
flooring loans.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Consumer Finance&#146;s net income decreased to $36.3&nbsp;million, down $0.3&nbsp;million, or 0.8%. Net
interest income of $78.5&nbsp;million was comparable to the prior
year with an increase of $0.4&nbsp;million.
Noninterest income remained relatively constant at $31.7&nbsp;million. Noninterest expense increased by
$2.4&nbsp;million or 6.1%, partially due to an increase in outside services. The provision for credit
losses increased $0.2&nbsp;million, or 2.0%.
</DIV>

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">31
</DIV>

<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt"><B>BancWest Corporation and Subsidiaries<BR>
MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL<BR>
CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B>

</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2004 compared with 2003</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Consumer
Finance&#146;s net income decreased to $36.6&nbsp;million, down $0.6&nbsp;million, or 1.6%. Net
interest income of $78.1&nbsp;million compared to $74.2&nbsp;million in the prior year, an increase of 5.3%.
The increase was primarily the result of increased interest income on higher loan balances.
Noninterest income decreased $4.0&nbsp;million, or 11.2%. The decrease was caused by lower gains on the
sale of mortgages in 2004 compared to 2003. Noninterest expense decreased $1.6&nbsp;million, or 3.9%.
The provision for credit losses increased $0.3&nbsp;million, or 3.2%.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<B>Commercial Banking</B>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2005 compared with 2004</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Commercial Banking&#146;s net income decreased to $16.9&nbsp;million, down $6.2&nbsp;million, or 26.8%,
primarily due to a $6.9&nbsp;million gain on the sale of a lease in the second quarter of 2004. Net
interest income decreased $2.0&nbsp;million, or 5.8%. Noninterest income decreased $7.9&nbsp;million, or
69.9%, due to the gain on sale of a lease in the second quarter of 2004. Noninterest expense
increased $0.4&nbsp;million, or 4.1%, compared to the same period in the prior year.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Average assets of $1.3&nbsp;billion were 12.0% higher than the prior year.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2004 compared with 2003</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Commercial Banking&#146;s net income decreased to $23.1&nbsp;million, down $1.7&nbsp;million, or 6.9%. Net
interest income decreased $8.2&nbsp;million, or 19.3%, primarily due to lower earning assets.
Noninterest income increased $0.6&nbsp;million, or 5.6%, partly due to a $6.9&nbsp;million gain on the sale of
a lease in the second quarter of 2004, partially offset by a $4.1&nbsp;million net gain on sale of the
net investment in a lease and a gain on sale of low income housing investments in 2003.
Noninterest expense decreased $3.1&nbsp;million, or 24.2%. The decrease was partly due to a $4.1
million pretax reduction in net investments of certain leverage leases in 2003. The provision for
credit losses of $0.7&nbsp;million decreased $4.1&nbsp;million, or 85.4%, due to an improvement in credit
quality.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<B>Wealth Management</B>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2005 compared with 2004</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Wealth Management segment&#146;s net income of $2.2&nbsp;million increased $0.3&nbsp;million from 2004.
Noninterest income increased by $0.7&nbsp;million, or 2.6% over the prior year. Noninterest expense
increased by $0.6&nbsp;million, or 2.5% compared to the same period in the prior year.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2004 compared with 2003</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The
Wealth Management segment&#146;s net income of $1.9&nbsp;million increased $0.8&nbsp;million from 2003.
Noninterest income of $27.2&nbsp;million increased by $1.3&nbsp;million, or 5.0%. Noninterest expense
increased $0.2&nbsp;million, or 0.9% compared to the same period in the prior year.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt"><B>BancWest Corporation and Subsidiaries<BR>
MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL<BR>
CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B>

</DIV>
<DIV align="left">
<A name="130"></A>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>SECURITIES AVAILABLE FOR SALE</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;A significant portion of the $2.5&nbsp;billion, or 31.1% increase in securities available for sale
from December&nbsp;31, 2004 to December&nbsp;31, 2005 was due to the acquisition of Commercial Federal with
the remaining increase due to purchases of securities.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company focuses on the following four objectives for its available-for-sale portfolio:
</DIV>

<DIV style="margin-top: 6pt">
<TABLE width="100%" border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" style="font-size: 10pt">

<TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="4%" style="background: transparent">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD width="2%" nowrap align="left"><B>&#149;</B></TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>Support its need for liquidity to fund loans or to meet unexpected deposit runoff.
Liquidity can be met by having investments with relatively short maturities and/or a high
degree of marketability.</TD>
</TR>

<TR>
    <TD style="font-size: 6pt">&nbsp;</TD>
</TR><TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="4%" style="background: transparent">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD width="2%" nowrap align="left"><B>&#149;</B></TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>Act as a vehicle to make meaningful shifts in the Company&#146;s overall interest rate risk
profile.</TD>
</TR>

<TR>
    <TD style="font-size: 6pt">&nbsp;</TD>
</TR><TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="4%" style="background: transparent">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD width="2%" nowrap align="left"><B>&#149;</B></TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>Provide collateral to secure the Company&#146;s public and private funds-taking activities
in addition to supporting other related Treasury strategies.</TD>
</TR>

<TR>
    <TD style="font-size: 6pt">&nbsp;</TD>
</TR><TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="4%" style="background: transparent">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD width="2%" nowrap align="left"><B>&#149;</B></TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>Provide the maximum level of after-tax earnings consistent with the safety factors of
quality, maturity, liquidity and risk diversification.</TD>
</TR>

</TABLE>
</DIV>
<DIV align="left">
<A name="131"></A>
</DIV>

</TABLE>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The loan and lease portfolio is the largest component of total earning assets and
accounts for the greatest portion of total interest income. In 2005, we continued our efforts to
diversify our loan and lease portfolio, both geographically and by industry. Our overall growth in
loan and lease volume came primarily from the acquisitions of Commercial Federal in the fourth
quarter of 2005 and Community First and USDB in the fourth quarter of 2004, purchases of
residential loans and internal growth.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>Commercial, Financial and Agricultural Loans</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We seek to maintain reasonable levels of risk in commercial, financial and agricultural
lending by following prudent underwriting guidelines primarily based on cash flow. Most commercial,
financial and agricultural loans are collateralized and/or supported by guarantors judged to have
adequate net worth. We make unsecured loans to customers based on character, net worth, liquidity
and repayment ability.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>Real Estate Loans</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Real estate loans represent the largest category of our loan portfolio, comprising 53.4% of
total loans and leases at December&nbsp;31, 2005, compared with 45.6% at December&nbsp;31, 2004. This
increase was primarily due to the acquisition of Commercial Federal, whose principal subsidiary was
a Federal Savings Bank, which added approximately $5.1&nbsp;billion in real estate loans. A significant
portion of the increase was also due to customers taking advantage of the low interest rate
environment and greater loan purchases by the Company during the year. Real estate loans are
divided among several diversified categories, including commercial and industrial
</DIV>

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">33
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt"><B>BancWest Corporation and Subsidiaries<BR>
MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL<BR>
CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B>

</DIV>
<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">loans where real estate collateral is pledged as an additional source of repayment of the
loans, home mortgage, home equity loans and lines and real estate construction and development.
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Real estate loans represented 45.6% and 43.2% of total loans and leases at December&nbsp;31, 2004
and 2003, respectively. Real estate loans increased 34.0%, or $3.8&nbsp;billion, primarily from the
acquisitions in the fourth quarter of 2004. A significant portion of the increase is also due to
customers taking advantage of the low interest rate environment and greater loan purchases during
the current year.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We seek to maintain reasonable levels of risk in real estate lending by financing projects
selectively, by adhering to prudent underwriting guidelines and by closely monitoring general
economic conditions affecting local real estate markets. In purchasing existing residential real
estate loans, we are able to diversify our geographic exposure.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<B>Multifamily and commercial real estate loans. </B>We analyze each application to assess the
project&#146;s economic viability, the loan-to-value ratio of the real estate securing the financing and
the underlying financial strength of the borrower. In this type of lending, we will generally: (1)
lend no more than 80% of the appraised value of the underlying project or property; and, (2)
require a minimum debt service ratio of 1.15.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<B>Single-family residential loans. </B>We will generally lend no more than 80% of the appraised
value of the underlying property. Although the majority of our loans adhere to that limit, loans
made in excess of that limit are generally covered by third-party mortgage insurance that reduces
our equivalent risk to an 80% loan-to-appraised-value ratio.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<B>Home equity loans. </B>We generally lend up to 75% of appraised value or tax assessed value for
first mortgages, otherwise 80-100% depending on the amount of the loan. The debt-to-income ratio
should not exceed 45% and a good credit history is required.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;As of February&nbsp;2006, approximately $439&nbsp;million of home equity loans that were acquired in the
Commercial Federal acquisition have loan-to-value ratios of 90% or greater. We also have
additional commitments related to unfunded home equity lines of credit of approximately $72
million. While the future performance of these loans and lines of credit is not known, there is a
possibility that they will not perform as well as those with lower loan-to-value ratios. The
performance of these loans is partially dependent on the properties&#146; geographical locations. These
loans are primarily located in Midwestern states.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>Consumer Loans</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Consumer loans consist primarily of open- and closed-end direct and indirect loans for
personal, automobile, recreational vehicle, pleasure boat and household purchases. We seek to
maintain reasonable levels of risk in consumer lending by following prudent underwriting
guidelines, which include an evaluation of: (1)&nbsp;personal credit history; (2)&nbsp;personal cash flow;
and (3)&nbsp;collateral value based on existing market conditions.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Consumer loans, including financing of automobiles, recreational vehicles and pleasure boats,
totaled 24.4% of total loans and leases at December&nbsp;31, 2005, compared with 28.3% in 2004. The
balance increased $1.4&nbsp;billion, or 15.2%, from last year primarily due to continued confidence in
the consumer market and attractive interest rates. A significant portion of the increase was also
due to the acquisition of Commercial Federal.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Consumer loans, including financing of automobiles, recreational vehicles and pleasure boats,
totaled 28.3% of total loans and leases at December&nbsp;31, 2004, compared with 28.6% in 2003. The
balance increased $1.9&nbsp;billion, or 25.9%, from 2003. This increase was primarily due to higher
confidence in the consumer market and attractive interest rates. A significant portion of the
increase was also due to the acquisitions of Community First and USDB. Low interest rates made
purchase financing for automobiles more attractive than leasing.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>Lease Financing</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In 2005 lease financing represented 5.0% of total loans and leases as compared to 6.5% in
2004, and 9.4% in 2003. Consumer lease financing has experienced only modest growth in response to
the relatively low interest rate environment which has changed consumer preferences away from lease
financing.
</DIV>

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">34
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt"><B>BancWest Corporation and Subsidiaries<BR>
MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL<BR>
CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B>

</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>Loan and Lease Concentrations</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Loan and lease concentrations exist when there are loans to multiple borrowers who are engaged
in similar activities and thus would be impacted by the same economic or other conditions. At
December&nbsp;31, 2005, we did not have a concentration of loans and leases greater than 10% of total
loans and leases, which was not otherwise disclosed as a category in the table above.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The loan and lease portfolio is predominately located in California, Hawaii, and other states
in the Western United States. We also lend, to a lesser extent, nationally and in Guam and Saipan.
The risk inherent in the portfolio is dependent upon both the economic stability of the areas in
which we lend and the financial well-being and creditworthiness of the borrowers.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>Loan and Lease Maturities</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The contractual maturities of loans and leases do not necessarily reflect the actual
maturities of our loan and lease portfolio. In our experience, the average life of residential real
estate and consumer loans is substantially less than their contractual terms because borrowers
prepay loans.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In general, the average life of real estate loans tends to increase when current interest
rates exceed rates on existing loans. In contrast, borrowers are more likely to prepay loans when
current interest rates are below the rates on existing loans. The volume of such prepayments
depends upon changes in both the absolute level of interest rates, the relationship between fixed
and adjustable-rate loans and the relative values of the underlying collateral. As a result, the
average life of our fixed-rate real estate loans has varied widely.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;At December&nbsp;31, 2005, loans and leases with contractual maturities of over one year were
comprised of fixed-rate loans totaling $20.9&nbsp;billion and floating or adjustable-rate loans totaling
$12.7&nbsp;billion.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The following table sets forth the contractual maturities of our loan and lease portfolio by
category at December&nbsp;31, 2005. Demand loans are included as due within one year.
</DIV>
<DIV align="center">
<TABLE style="font-size: 10pt" cellspacing="0" border="0" cellpadding="0" width="80%">

    <TD nowrap align="left"><SUP style="font-size: 85%; vertical-align: text-top">(1)</SUP></TD>
    <TD>&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>Includes a $645 thousand Troubled Debt Restructuring acquired in connection with
the acquisition of Commercial Federal.</TD>
</TR>

<TR valign="top">
    <TD nowrap align="left"><SUP style="font-size: 85%; vertical-align: text-top">(2)</SUP></TD>
    <TD>&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>Represents loans and leases which are past due 90&nbsp;days or more as to principal
and/or interest, are still accruing interest, are adequately collateralized and are in the
process of collection.</TD>
</TR>

</TABLE>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>Nonperforming Assets</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We generally place a loan or lease on nonaccrual status when we believe that collection of
principal or interest has become doubtful or when loans or leases are 90&nbsp;days past due as to
principal or interest, unless they are well secured and in the process of collection.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Consumer loans and leases are subject to our general policies regarding nonaccrual loans and
substantially all past-due consumer loans and leases are charged off upon reaching a predetermined
delinquency status varying from 120 to 180&nbsp;days, depending on product type.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;When we place a loan or lease on nonaccrual status, previously accrued and uncollected
interest is reversed against interest income of the current period. When we receive a cash payment
on a nonaccrual loan, we apply it as a reduction of the principal balance when we have doubts about
the ultimate collection of the principal. Otherwise, we record such payments as income.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Nonaccrual loans and leases are generally returned to accrual status when they: (1)&nbsp;become
current as to principal and interest and have demonstrated a sustained period of payment
performance or (2)&nbsp;become both well secured and in the process of collection.
</DIV>

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">36
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->

<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt"><B>BancWest Corporation and Subsidiaries<BR>
MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL<BR>
CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B>

</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Nonperforming assets at December&nbsp;31, 2005 were $223.2&nbsp;million, or 0.51%, of total loans and
leases, other real estate owned, and repossessed personal property, as compared to 0.45% at
December&nbsp;31, 2004 and 0.59% at December&nbsp;31, 2003. Nonperforming assets at December&nbsp;31, 2005 were
0.34% of total assets, compared to 0.29% at December&nbsp;31, 2004 and 0.39% at December&nbsp;31, 2003.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2005 compared with 2004 </U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Total nonaccrual loans and leases increased $67.5&nbsp;million. Nonaccrual loans for commercial,
financial and agricultural lending decreased $5.1&nbsp;million due to the payoff of an agricultural loan
in November&nbsp;2005 of $9.5&nbsp;million, partially offset by an increase in loans of $3&nbsp;million from the acquisition
of Commercial Federal in December&nbsp;2005. Foreign nonaccruing loans decreased by $3.2&nbsp;million due to
the resolution of a large problem relationship. Our overall foreign loan portfolio, composed
primarily of loans in Guam and Saipan, represents a relatively small component (0.5%) of our total
loan portfolio. In addition, there was an increase in nonaccrual real estate loans of $74.2
million. The increase was the result of our acquisition of Commercial Federal whose loan portfolio
consisted primarily of real estate loans.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2004 compared with 2003</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Total nonaccrual loans and leases decreased $8.6&nbsp;million in 2004 as compared with 2003.
Nonaccrual loans for commercial, financial and agricultural lending decreased $14.3&nbsp;million due to
the resolution of problem relationships. Foreign nonaccruing loans decreased by $2.2&nbsp;million to
$4.1&nbsp;million. Our overall foreign loan portfolio, composed primarily of loans in Guam and Saipan,
represents a relatively small component (1.2%) of our total loan portfolio. In addition, there was
a decrease in nonaccrual residential real estate loans of $1.3&nbsp;million resulting from the
resolution of problem relationships. These decreases were partially offset by an increase in total
nonaccrual commercial and construction real estate loans of $8.3&nbsp;million, which was partially due
to the acquisitions of Community First and USDB in the fourth quarter of 2004.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The following table presents information related to nonaccrual and restructured loans and
leases as of December&nbsp;31, 2005:

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;First Hawaiian Bank has credit exposure to an airline of approximately $4.9&nbsp;million as of
February&nbsp;28, 2006. The borrower filed for Chapter&nbsp;11 reorganization on December&nbsp;30, 2004 and at
that time, First Hawaiian downgraded all of the borrower&#146;s loans and leases to doubtful and placed
all loans and leases on nonaccrual status. Based on management&#146;s analysis of the borrower&#146;s
collateral, no specific reserve is required on the borrower&#146;s outstanding balances. The borrower
emerged from bankruptcy on February&nbsp;17, 2006 as an operating airline. First Hawaiian&#146;s credit
exposure continues on a fully cash collateralized basis and all contractual payments of principal
and interest have been made through December&nbsp;31, 2005.
</DIV>

</TABLE>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The provision for loan and lease losses is based on management&#146;s judgment as to the
adequacy of the Allowance. Management uses a systematic methodology to determine the related
provision for loan and lease losses. The determination of the adequacy of the Allowance is
ultimately one of management judgment, which includes consideration of many factors such as: (1)
the amount of problem loans and leases existing at the balance sheet date (whether or not
specifically identified at that date); (2)&nbsp;net charge-off experience; (3)&nbsp;changes in the
composition of the loan and lease portfolio by type and location of loans and leases; (4)&nbsp;changes
in overall loan and lease risk profile and quality; (5)&nbsp;general economic factors; (6)&nbsp;specific
regional economic factors; and (7)&nbsp;the fair value of collateral.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Using this methodology, we allocate the Allowance to individual loans and leases and to
categories of loans and leases representing probable losses based on available information. At
least quarterly, we conduct internal credit analyses to determine which loans and leases are
impaired. As a result, we allocate specific amounts of the Allowance to individual loan and lease
relationships. Each impaired relationship over $1,000,000 and classified substandard or doubtful is
evaluated quarterly on a case-by-case basis. Note 1 to the Consolidated Financial Statements
describes how we evaluate loans for impairment. Note 8 to the Consolidated Financial Statements
details additional information regarding the Allowance and impaired loans.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Some categories of loans and leases are not subjected to a loan-by-loan credit analysis.
Management makes an allocation to these categories based on our analysis of historic trends of
impairment and charge-offs of such loans and leases. Additionally, we allocate a portion of the
Allowance based on risk classifications of certain loan types. Some of the Allowance is not
allocated to specific impaired loans because of the subjective nature of the process of estimating
an adequate allowance for loan and lease losses, economic uncertainties and other factors.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The allocated component of the allowance increased from 74.1% of the total Allowance for loan
and lease losses at December&nbsp;31, 2004 to 86.0% at December&nbsp;31, 2005. The increase reflected
management&#146;s ongoing process of refining its allocation methodology with respect to specific loan
products and risk pools. Changes in the allocated loan and lease loss Allowance reflect
management&#146;s judgment concerning the effect of trends in borrower performance and recent economic
activity on portfolio performance.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The unallocated component of the Allowance decreased from 25.9% of the total Allowance for
loan and lease losses at December&nbsp;31, 2004 to 14.0% at December&nbsp;31, 2005, primarily due to the
improvement in our loan portfolio&#146;s credit quality, which required less reliance on judgmental
assumptions built into the unallocated component of the Allowance.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We continually analyze our processes and portfolio in an attempt to mitigate risk within our
loan portfolio. While we have not specifically identified loans or leases that are currently losses
or potential problem loans (other than those identified in our discussion of nonperforming assets),
certain events make it probable that there are losses inherent in our portfolio. These events
include:
</DIV>

<DIV style="margin-top: 6pt">
<TABLE width="100%" border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" style="font-size: 10pt">

<TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="8%" style="background: transparent">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD width="3%" nowrap align="left"><B>&#149;</B></TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>While the economy within the United States appears to be improving, the economic
recovery has not yet fully taken hold and some areas remain sluggish. The
unemployment rate remains high and job creation is slower than anticipated. A lack
of strength in the labor market could negatively impact one of our key customer
groups, consumers, potentially resulting in a detrimental effect on the credit
quality of our loan and lease portfolio.</TD>
</TR>

</TABLE>
</DIV>

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">39
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt"><B>BancWest Corporation and Subsidiaries<BR>
MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL<BR>
CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B>

</DIV>

<DIV style="margin-top: 6pt">
<TABLE width="100%" border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" style="font-size: 10pt">

<TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="8%" style="background: transparent">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD width="3%" nowrap align="left"><B>&#149;</B></TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>Unsettled geopolitical events, including tensions in Iraq, could have an adverse
effect on the current economic environment. International disputes and other factors
could stall the economic recovery for an indeterminate amount of time, or even
prompt a return to economic slowdown in the United States.</TD>
</TR>

<TR>
    <TD style="font-size: 6pt">&nbsp;</TD>
</TR><TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="8%" style="background: transparent">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD width="3%" nowrap align="left"><B>&#149;</B></TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>Energy costs are increasing due to tension in the Middle East. As we experienced
during the energy crisis in California a few years ago, higher energy costs can
negatively impact the economic conditions of the markets we serve.</TD>
</TR>

<TR>
    <TD style="font-size: 6pt">&nbsp;</TD>
</TR><TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="8%" style="background: transparent">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD width="3%" nowrap align="left"><B>&#149;</B></TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>California is one of our key geographical markets. The economic slowdown
experienced in recent years was particularly severe in the technology field, which
is heavily based in California. The Californian economic slowdown, and other
external factors including the previously mentioned energy crisis, contributed to
the State experiencing a substantial budget deficit. Actions the State may, or may
not, take to address its deficit issue could affect customers the Company serves, or
the Company directly.</TD>
</TR>

</TABLE>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We will continue to closely monitor the current and potential impact that these factors have
on our loan and lease portfolio. Worsening economic conditions may warrant an increase in the
provision for credit losses in future periods.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>Net Charge-Offs</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2005 compared with 2004 </U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Net charge-offs were $59.3&nbsp;million, a decrease of $4.6&nbsp;million. Total loans and leases
charged off decreased $3.2&nbsp;million. This decrease was due to a $7.0&nbsp;million decrease in
charge-offs for commercial, financial and agricultural loans and a $7.9&nbsp;million decrease in lease
financing, partially offset by an increase of $9.8&nbsp;million in consumer loan charge-offs, primarily
due to the increased size of the consumer loan portfolio. The decrease in net charge-offs was
primarily due to the improved credit quality of our loan portfolio, as well as the continuing
strong economy in the western United States.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Net charge-offs were 0.17% of average loans and leases compared to 0.23%.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2004 compared with 2003</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Net charge-offs were $63.9&nbsp;million in 2004, a decrease of $9.8&nbsp;million as compared with 2003.
Total loans and leases charged off decreased $26.1&nbsp;million. This decrease was primarily due to a
$23.1&nbsp;million decrease in charge-offs for commercial, financial and agricultural loans, partially
offset by an increase of $2.1&nbsp;million in consumer loan charge-offs, primarily due to the increased
size of the consumer loan portfolio.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Net charge-offs in 2004 were 0.23% of average loans and leases compared to 0.30% in 2003.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>Allowance for Loan and Lease Losses</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In our judgment, the Allowance was adequate to absorb losses inherent in the loan and lease
portfolio at December&nbsp;31, 2005. However, changes in prevailing economic conditions in our markets
could result in changes in the level of nonperforming assets and charge-offs in the future and,
accordingly, changes in the Allowance. We will continue to closely monitor economic developments
and make adjustments to the Allowance accordingly.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The company uses the guidance in Statement of Position (SOP)&nbsp;03-3, <I>Accounting for Certain
Loans or Debt Securities Acquired in a Transfer, </I>to account for loans acquired where there is
deterioration in credit quality and it is probable that we would be unable to collect all
contractually required payments. In connection with our acquisition of Commercial Federal on
December&nbsp;2, 2005, the Company acquired loans where there was evidence of deterioration in credit
quality and it was probable that we would be unable to collect all contractually required payments.
These loans are on nonaccrual status and the Company recorded them at their estimated fair value
of $51.8&nbsp;million. In addition, the Company acquired a $76.2&nbsp;million allowance for loan and lease
losses from Commercial Federal.
</DIV>

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">40
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt"><B>BancWest Corporation and Subsidiaries<BR>
MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL<BR>
CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B>

</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Gulf States Hurricanes
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company has performed an evaluation of its exposure to potential loss as a result of the
devastation caused during 2005 by hurricanes Katrina, Rita and Wilma. At this time, the Company
does not believe that it has a significant exposure to loss as a result of the hurricanes.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2005 compared with 2004</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The allowance for loan and lease losses was $490.3&nbsp;million, an increase of $53.9&nbsp;million. The
increase was primarily due to the acquisition of Commercial Federal.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Allowance decreased to 2.54 times nonaccruing loans and leases (excluding 90&nbsp;days or more
past due accruing loans and leases) from 3.49 times, primarily resulting from the increase in
nonaccruing loans and leases.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<U>2004 compared with 2003</U>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The allowance for loan and lease losses was $436.4&nbsp;million, an increase of $44.7&nbsp;million. The
increase was primarily due to the acquisitions of Community First and USDB.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Allowance increased to 3.49 times nonaccruing loans and leases (excluding 90&nbsp;days or more
past due accruing loans and leases) from 2.93 times, primarily due to the decrease in nonaccruing
loans and leases.
</DIV>
<DIV align="left">
<A name="134"></A>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>DEPOSITS</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Deposits are the largest component of our total liabilities and accounted for 51.8% of total
interest expense during the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2005. At December&nbsp;31, 2005, total deposits were
$42.4&nbsp;billion, an increase of 26.2% over December&nbsp;31, 2004. The increase was mostly due to
increases in money market savings and time certificates of deposits resulting from our acquisition
of Commercial Federal, increases within our business time deposits, as well as internal growth.
Rates paid on deposits have increased based on new market conditions. Additional information on
our average deposit balances and rates paid is provided in Table 1: Average Balances, Interest
Income and Expense, and Yields and Rates (Taxable-Equivalent Basis).
</DIV>
<DIV align="left">
<A name="135"></A>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>CAPITAL</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Stockholder&#146;s equity totaled $6.8&nbsp;billion at December&nbsp;31, 2005, an increase of $1.0&nbsp;billion,
or 17.8%, from December&nbsp;31, 2004. The increase from December&nbsp;31, 2004 was due to an issuance of
common stock of the Company to BNP Paribas related to the acquisition of Commercial Federal and net
income for the last 12&nbsp;months.
</DIV>
<DIV align="left">
<A name="136"></A>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>INCOME TAXES</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The provision for income taxes represented 37.0%, 38.7%, and 38.3% of pretax income for 2005,
2004 and 2003, respectively. Further information on our income taxes is provided in Note 19 (Income
Taxes) to the Consolidated Financial Statements.
</DIV>

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">41
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt"><B>BancWest Corporation and Subsidiaries<BR>
MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL<BR>
CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B>

</DIV>
<DIV align="left">
<A name="137"></A>
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>OFF-BALANCE SHEET ARRANGEMENTS</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt"><B>Commitments and Guarantees</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In the normal course of business, we are a party to various off-balance sheet commitments
entered into to meet the financing needs of our customers. These financial instruments include
commitments to extend credit, standby and commercial letters of credit and commitments to purchase
or sell foreign currencies. These commitments involve, to varying degrees, elements of credit,
interest rate and foreign exchange rate risk. We also enter into commitments to provide funding
for our balance sheet and operations. These commitments include time deposits, short-term and
long-term borrowings, leases and other financial obligations.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company issues standby letters of credit, which include performance and financial
guarantees, on behalf of customers in connection with contracts between the customers and third
parties whereby the Company assures that the third parties will receive specified funds if
customers fail to meet their contractual obligations. Standby letters of credit totaled $854.6
million at December&nbsp;31, 2005, including financial guarantees of $781.8&nbsp;million that the Company had
issued or in which it purchased participations. Commercial and similar letters of credit totaled
$70.3&nbsp;million at December&nbsp;31, 2005. A major portion of all fees received from the issuance of
standby letters of credit are deferred and, at December&nbsp;31, 2005, were immaterial to the Company&#146;s
financial statements. If the counterparty to a commitment to extend credit or to a standby or
commercial letter of credit fails to perform, our exposure to loan and lease losses would be the
contractual notional amount. Since these commitments may expire without being drawn upon, the
total commitment amounts do not necessarily represent future cash flows. For more information on
our credit extension commitments please refer to Note 7 (Loans and Leases) to the Consolidated
Financial Statements.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company enters into indemnification agreements in the ordinary course of business under
which the Company agrees to indemnify third parties against any damages, losses and expenses
incurred in connection with legal and other proceedings arising from relationships or transactions
with the Company. These relationships or transactions include those arising from service as a
director or officer of the Company, underwriting agreements relating to the Company&#146;s securities,
securities lending, acquisition agreements, and various other business transactions or
arrangements. Because the extent of the Company&#146;s obligations under these indemnification
agreements depends entirely upon the occurrence of future events, the Company&#146;s potential future
liability under these agreements is not determinable.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>Retained or Contingent Interest</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company has provided liquidity facilities for our SBA loans. We retained a portion of the
interest in the loans thereby providing a cushion to the senior interests in the event that a
portion of the loans becomes uncollectible. Total outstanding risk is $5.0&nbsp;million and has been
recorded in the Company&#146;s Consolidated Financial Statements.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;While not a major liquidity source, the Company sells residential mortgages and other loans
and has in prior years sold securitized mortgage loans. Retained interests in securitized assets,
including debt securities, are initially recorded at their allocated carrying amounts based on the
relative fair value of assets sold and retained. Retained interests in interest only strips are
subsequently carried at fair value, which is generally estimated based on the present value of
expected cash flows, calculated using management&#146;s best estimates of key assumptions, including
loan and lease losses, loan repayment speeds and discount rates commensurate with risks involved.
Gains and losses related to the sales of retained interests are recorded in noninterest income.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Off-balance sheet agreements are subject to the same credit and market risk limitations as
those of assets and liabilities recorded on the balance sheet. Our testing to measure and monitor
this risk, using net interest income simulations and market value of equity analysis, is conducted
quarterly.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 12pt"><B>Variable Interest Entities</B>
</DIV>


<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company holds variable interests in certain special purpose entities that are not required
to be consolidated. See Note 5 (Variable Interest Entities (VIEs)) to the Consolidated Financial
Statements for additional information.
</DIV>

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">42
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In the normal course of business, we enter into contractual arrangements whereby we
commit to future purchases of products or services from unaffiliated parties. Obligations that are
legally binding agreements whereby we agree to purchase products or services with a defined minimum
quantity at a fixed, minimum or variable price over a specified period of time are defined as
purchase obligations. These obligations are categorized by their contractual due dates in the table
above. We may, at our option, prepay certain of these borrowings prior to their maturity date.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The most significant of our vendor contracts include communication services, marketing and
software contracts. Other liabilities include our obligations related to funded pension plans.
Obligations to these plans are based on the current and projected obligations of the plans and
performance of the plans&#146; assets. In addition, Other liabilities include a commitment to pay the
former shareholders of Trinity Capital Corporation one payment of $1.5&nbsp;million in 2006, which was
made in January&nbsp;2006 finalizing our obligations under the purchase agreement.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;First Hawaiian Bank processes credit card transactions and has loans outstanding to an
airline, which filed a voluntary petition to reorganize under Chapter&nbsp;11 of the Bankruptcy Code on
December&nbsp;30, 2004. The airline emerged from bankruptcy on February&nbsp;17, 2006 as an operating
airline.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Under the rules of VISA and MasterCard, First Hawaiian has certain contingent liabilities for
transactions processed and could become responsible to pay charge backs with respect to tickets and
coupons purchased should the airline not honor those tickets and coupons. Since the filing of its
Chapter&nbsp;11 petition, the airline has continued to operate and honor all tickets and coupons issued
before its filings.
</DIV>

<DIV align="left" style="font-size: 10pt; margin-top: 6pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;As of January&nbsp;31, 2006, First Hawaiian estimates that the cost of tickets and coupons
purchased by the airline&#146;s customers through VISA and MasterCard, but as yet unused, was
approximately $28.1&nbsp;million. As of January&nbsp;31, 2006, First Hawaiian held cash or cash equivalents
as collateral security for its potential charge back exposure to the airline customers in the
amount of $24.1&nbsp;million. Based on the current circumstances of the airline and other information
currently available to First Hawaiian, management does not believe it is probable that the Company
will incur material loss as a result of charge backs from customers of the airline. A reserve for
a portion of the exposure has been recorded in the Company&#146;s financial statements as of December
31, 2005.
</DIV>

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">43
</DIV>
